text.title: Physical Design: Indexes, Naming, and Anti-patterns
text.summary: Physical modeling decisions that most affect runtime performance: index strategy, partitioning for large tables, naming conventions, and common anti-patterns that cause production issues.

rule.statement: Design indexes based on actual query patterns before creating them. Over-indexing slows writes; under-indexing slows reads. Every index must be justified by a specific query.
rule.rationale: Each index is maintained on every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. A table with 20 indexes on infrequently queried columns has 20x the write overhead with no read benefit for most operations.

list.item: B-tree (default): equality and range queries on scalar columns
Composite B-tree: multi-column WHERE clauses — column order matches query predicate selectivity
Partial index: index only rows matching a condition (WHERE is_active = TRUE) — smaller, faster
Covering index (INCLUDE): attach extra columns to avoid table lookups for hot queries
GIN: full-text search (tsvector), JSONB fields, array columns
BRIN: monotonically increasing columns (timestamps, serial IDs) — very small, only for sorted data

antipattern.description: Index on every column "just in case"
antipattern.reason: Multiplies write overhead; PostgreSQL's query planner stops using indexes beyond ~10% of table rows anyway

rule.statement: Consider partitioning when a table is expected to exceed 50 million rows or when retention-based deletion is required. Partition on the column used in the most common WHERE clause (usually a timestamp or tenant ID).
rule.rationale: Partition pruning lets the query planner scan only relevant partitions. Partition-based DROP is orders of magnitude faster than DELETE for data retention.

list.item: Range partitioning: time-series tables (partition by month/week)
Hash partitioning: even data distribution when no natural range key exists
List partitioning: discrete values (tenant_id, region, status)

section.title: Naming Conventions

rule.statement: Establish and enforce naming conventions before the schema grows. Consistency allows tooling, code generation, and team communication to work without friction.
rule.rationale: Mixed naming conventions (camelCase vs snake_case, tbl_ prefix vs no prefix, id vs user_id for PKs) create constant cognitive overhead and break ORM auto-mapping.

list.item: Tables: singular snake_case nouns — user, product, order_item (not users, tbl_user)
Booleans: is_, has_, can_ prefix — is_active, has_subscription, can_export

section.reference: PostgreSQL table with all conventions applied

antipattern.description: Using TEXT/VARCHAR for all columns regardless of actual data shape
antipattern.reason: Prevents database-level constraint enforcement and degrades index efficiency; use INT for IDs, TIMESTAMPTZ for datetimes, DECIMAL for money, BOOLEAN for flags
Makes rows unaddressable; breaks replication, ORMs, and bulk operations

antipattern.description: Storing formatted data as strings (dates as "Jan 20 2025", money as "$149.99")
antipattern.reason: Prevents sorting, filtering, and arithmetic; format in the application layer, store in native types
Hides required business constraints; NULL in SQL has three-valued logic that breaks equality comparisons
Usually indicates multiple concerns in one table; split into a core table and satellite tables or consider vertical partitioning
